text: The laparoscopic view shows the hiatal hernia with the stomach protruding through the diaphragm into the chest. The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector to free the stomach from its attachments to the diaphragm, starting on the left side and then moving to the right crura. The esophagus is dissected from the chest cavity, allowing the stomach to be reduced back into the abdominal cavity and the esophagus to be lengthened. The greater curvature of the stomach is mobilized by dividing blood vessels, preparing for the fundoplication wrap to prevent acid reflux.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector to sever the final attachments of the stomach to the esophagus and splenic vessels. The mediastinum is inspected, and residual attachments to the left of the esophagus are freed to achieve full mobilization of the esophagus.
The surgeon completes the closure of the diaphragmatic crura using interrupted sutures, snugly securing the diaphragm around the esophagus to prevent stomach protrusion into the chest cavity.

The surgeon grabs the greater curvature of the stomach and wraps it posterior to the esophagus, ensuring it is not twisted and reaches under no tension. A dilator is placed through the wrap to check for tightness, preventing dysphagia. Three interrupted sutures are used to secure the wrap, with knots tied and reinforced, creating a wrap approximately two and a half centimeters in length.
The completed wrap is shown with a dilator left in place to test its tightness, ensuring it is not too constrictive. A cartoon diagram illustrates the final appearance of the procedure.
